speaker: interviewer
text: OK like this?

speaker: interviewer
text: You want a close shot on that, and then pull out.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then adjust-- did you see me on it too? They want me--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. No, we've got you there.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Just give me the go-ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: June 29, 1995. Interview with Mr. Harry Spitz. Interviewer, Maximilian Lerner. Phoenix, Arizona. Interview conducted in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: June 29, 1995. Interview with Mr. Harry Spitz. Interviewer, Maximilian Lerner. Phoenix, Arizona. Interview conducted in English. Mr. Spitz, please tell us when and where you were born. My name is Harry B. Spitz, and I was born in Mukachevo in Czechoslovakia. And it's a town of about 36,000 people, and over 18,000 was Jewish people in town.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was born March 1st, 1923. And I come of a family with my parents and five children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your family orthodox?

speaker: interviewee
text: My family was, yeah, it was kosher. It was orthodox Jewish people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did your parents do?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents-- my father had a general store of all kind, porcelain, and pots and pans, and glass, and crystal, and groceries. You name it, we had it. It was a huge store. And we had quite a few employees. And my father run the store. And we did very well, thanks God, but--

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of a home did you live in?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a very beautiful home.

speaker: interviewer
text: A house?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a house, yeah. We had a house. And, and we had a very, a very nice balbatish home.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you grew up, you went to school in Mukachevo?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to school, to Czech schools. In the morning, and in the afternoon I went to cheider, to Hebrew school.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did your mother work?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother had a business of her own. And she used to make wigs for ladies, wigs, and also for men, me toupees. And she mostly was for-- in the beginning, it was only religious people, which the Jewish girls got married, they had to cut their hair in the-- for the-- for-- in the orthodox religion. And they used to wear a wig or a kerchief on their head. And they used to come from all over town and, and also from neighboring towns and from all over Czechoslovakia to come to my mother. My mother had about 10 girls working for her. She was very, very busy. And she had a very successful business. And she was like an artist. When she made a wig, you could never tell that the wig was a wig. It looks like the original hair. It was just beautiful.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, when-- in 1938, what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1938, the trouble started when the Hungarians came and they took over part of Carpathian. It was used to call the Carpathian Carpatzkarus. The Carpathian, it was-- before the war, it used to be belonging to Austria-Hungary. And then after the first World War, the Czechoslovakian regime took it over. It was called [NON-ENGLISH]. And then the Hungarians always claimed that this is their land and they want it back. And Hitler actually gave them the land to take back, to occupy the Carpathian, and the Hungarian army marched in. And all the people, the Czech people and the Slovak people, cried and packed up and they left town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, when was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was in 1938.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the--

speaker: interviewee
text: In, in 1938. And I don't know exactly. In the beginning, it was something like [NON-ENGLISH] time, March or April time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: We stayed, of course. The Jewish people weren't too much affected in the beginning because we got along very well with the people and in the public, and the neighboring towns, and the people, and the Hungarians. And we spoke even their language very well. So we got very well along with them. And so, but it started beginning to get a lot of problems because who were came to town and married our own people, like Czech or Hungarian Jews. And they came over from the border towns, for like from Poland, so they started take the Polish people, like the Polish people they came in.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they, they grabbed them and, and they tried to deport them. We never know what happened to them because they took those people and we never know where they taken them. And not only they take the person, but they took the whole families. The wives and the children, and sometimes even if the in-laws wanted to go with them, they took them, even the in-laws. And these were the Hungarians who did that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That, that was done by the Hungarians, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in 1939 when the war started in September of 1939, how were you affected?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1939 when the war started, the, the Jews were kind of depressed. They-- you couldn't go in the streets very well, because you heard remarks, call you Jews should go home, or Jew go to Palestine, and stuff like that. And it was kind of, kind of depressed. You weren't, you weren't free to move around like you used to. So you had to be, you know, cautious and careful not to make waves to get in trouble.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you first see German troops?

speaker: interviewee
text: We seen-- the first time we seen German troops was in 1942. And they, they-- but there weren't much German troops because the Hungarian Gendarme was kind of cahoots with the German soldiers and the German troops. And they took over the town like the, the running of the town, or the, the execution of the people who they wanted to deport or to get first. And it started getting worse and worse all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any specific laws against Jews under the Hungarians?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not in the beginning, no. Later on, it became that the Jews weren't allowed to go shopping in the town, or only stay in certain areas like a ghetto. They certainly-- parts of the town was like a Jewish-- was just Jewish people living there. It was called the Yiddish Garte, Jewish street. And, and only, only Jews were going-- allowed to go in that part of the town to get their produce and their groceries. And it became quite very, very obvious and very bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: And during that time, you stayed in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: During that time, I was a young boy. And I was working as an apprentice for a locksmith guy. And I became a journeyman, and I also learned about plumbing and all kind different steel work. And we used to-- I used to make all kinds of stuff. And they didn't know of course that I'm Jewish because the way I was dressed, and with all the tools I used to carry around, and I used to have a bicycle. And I used to have a bicycle with a basket on it. And in the basket, I used to carry all my tools to do all the repairs and fixing. The people used to call me to fix all kinds, shutters, locks, and plumbing. Sometime in the winter time it froze, the pipes froze from the water, and it busted. When it started melting away, it got a little warmer, so we had to go replace, cut out those parts of the pipes and fix it. So I was working as a plumber and a, and a locksmith. And, you know, I was quite busy, but--

speaker: interviewer
text: And in 1942, the Germans--

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1942, they-- we-- they conducted me in the, in the, in the camps, like a working camp. In the beginning--

speaker: interviewer
text: A labor camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the labor camp. And they took me to a, to a city called [NON-ENGLISH]. It's deep inside in Hungary. And they conducted me. They picked us because of course I was mechanically inclined. So this was called the mechanical brigade. The whole-- all the Jewish boys who were in my, my unit were all mechanics, auto mechanics, all kind different trades. They all had trades, even carpenters and, and, and bricklayers. Whatever, they were builders. They were all mechanically inclined. So they took us to that mechanical labor camp, and they gave us once a day a little soup and a piece of bread. And we slept in barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who ran the camp, the Hungarians?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Hungarians, yeah. The Hungarians. Was strictly ran by the Hungarians. We didn't see any German people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you-- what happened? What kind of work did you do in this camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: In, in-- later on in 1943, they took me to a different city. It was-- they-- it was called [NON-ENGLISH]. It's an airplane factory. And they used to make their airplanes and also helicopters for the Army, for the defense. They, they used to say we're making for defense. We're working for the Hungarian government. And we ship those planes and helicopters to Germany. They need it for their fatherland, to defend the fatherland.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we worked there for about three, four months. But it was quite very dangerous because they used to come three, four times a day to bomb the place. The British used to come. And they-- after four months, they completely demolished the place with the bombs. And there is a lot of Jewish boys and girls got killed from the bombs and the shrapnels.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the Jewish boys-- the siren used to come on, and the Jewish boys and girls weren't allowed to go in those bunkers. They were built bunkers, but they were working other Christian people there, too. So they were always hiding out in the bunkers to be safe from the bombs. But the Jews weren't allowed to go there because they said, that's your own people coming. So you don't have to go there. They wouldn't let you in. So in-- after it got demolished completely almost, they couldn't do any more labor there or work there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they set us up in a transport again, and they took us to a place in an island that's called [NON-ENGLISH]. It was owned by a Jewish-- a very well-to-do Jewish millionaire. It was a steel factory. And they converted the steel factory into ammunition factory. It was taken away from this Jewish man?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken away from the Jewish people. The man was probably imprisoned or whatever. We don't even know what happened to him. He was a multi multimillionaire, a manufacturer of steel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they took it away?

speaker: interviewee
text: And they-- the government confiscated everything, this house, his property, his factory. And, and they, and they converted the factory, as that steel, they were pouring steel. So they converted it. And they brought a specialist to make ammunition and all kind of different bombs for the, for the war. And they took us there, and we were about 500 or 600 Jewish boys and girls. And we worked there day and night. And at night, we got a little plate of soup with a slice of bread. And we slept in barracks. We weren't allowed to leave the factory. They had in behind the factory huge wooden barracks made out of wooden crates, like crates. And we had to sleep in those barracks. And they didn't give us any beds or anything, not even bunkers. We had to sleep on the concrete, on the plain floor concrete floors.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was lucky because I could-- I was always an organizer, so I could steal a board and sleep on a piece of board and put an old blanket on the board, and I could a little relax more. But some boys and girls weren't that active or very organized. So they didn't-- they were always afraid whatever they going to do, it's going to be bad. I said, don't be afraid. So what worse can happen to you? I said, take it. If you need a piece of board, take it and sleep on it. So-- and I tried to help them out always, you know, to get them organized. And sometimes I even went out and got it for those people, but it was quite bad.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was working there for about three months. And I got so sick and tired of that place. And we weren't allowed to take much rest. We kept working all day. They had foremans used to go out and watch over us, that we don't fool around on the job.

speaker: interviewer
text: These were all Hungarians?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were all Hungarians. They were all Hungarian Nazis. They called themselves [NON-ENGLISH]. They used to wear a, a cross, like a special cross with a special uniform on it because they were in charge. They were not plain Hungarians. They were, they were almost even worse than the Germans, because they gave them authority, and they, they wanted to show off with their authority. So they were very bad. If you didn't do your job, they even hit you real hard. And they punished you with all kind, different things. And sometimes if you, if they, if they found it that you didn't do your job right, you didn't get your food. He says, oh, you go on the other side. You're not going to get your meal today, so which was very bad because people used to be very hungry and even thirsty. Even water was hard to come by, not only food.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I decided I'm going to just do something and try to get away from this place. So I made friends with a Christian boy. He was only about a year or two years older than I am. And he was very friendly to me, because he seen I'm, I'm sincere and I'm doing a good job, and I knew he can depend on me. We started talking, and he says to me-- You were about 20 years old at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was only 19. 19. And he says to me, Harry, you look like a nice guy. And I happen to like you. Can I tell you something? I said, sure. I says, we friends, aren't we? He says, I'll bring you some of my clothes in next time I come to work tomorrow. Take off your shirt with the yellow star and that yellow band on your jacket and all that, and put on my clothes. And when we go out from the factory, people used to go home. After 6 o'clock in the evening, the Christian workers used to go home. They used to come in at 9 or 8 o'clock in the morning and go home 6:00. They worked only eight, nine hours and they went home. And we used to have to stay there. They assembled us and put us back to the barracks. I says, OK, I would appreciate that if you could help me. So he did, and I changed my clothes. And sure enough, I-- he says to me, take some tools in your hands, and put some black coal on your face. Make yourself look like you were working the day with the coals and the steel. With the steel, we had to pull the steel, hard work. And like the Christian boys worked there. He says you stand in line behind me. And when we march out from the factory, you can follow me out. Sure enough, I did it. And luckily I-- they didn't stop me or anything. And I walked out with all the Christian workers and came out from the factory. And he says to me, Harry, I brought you out from the factory, but I can't take you to my house, or I can't take you with me. But from now on, you have to be on your own. You have to see what you can do for yourself.

speaker: interviewer
text: I says, Pista-- his name was Pista. I says, Pista, what, what can I do? I don't, I don't have much proper identifications, just some old papers and something I carried out in a backpack. I was hiding it.

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, I'm sorry, but I don't know what to tell you. Go into Budapest, up to Buda, and find out if there are some people who are trying to help Jewish people. Because I heard there is a Swedish and a Swiss consul. They are stay trying to help Jewish people, and they put them in these homes where they have under Swedish protection. They have a Swedish flag on the building, and it's supposed to be under Swedish protection.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, when was that? This was in 1944?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: What month?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was sometime in March, just before Passover. Yeah, March 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. How far was, was this place from Budapest?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was about 30 kilometers. It was an island.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get to Budapest?

speaker: interviewee
text: I hitchhiked, and I walked, and I was hiding out in every way. So finally I got to Budapest by hitchhiking. What you call, farmers, used to go with horse and wagon. So I used to offer them help. I said, I could help him. And if you want me, can I go in with you to town. Because they carry their produce into Budapest to sell their produce, these farmers.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he said, OK, if you're going to help me, you can come with me. You can follow the wagon. And if you get too tired, you can sit on the wagon. In the beginning, they wouldn't let you to go on the wagon. But once you get friendly with them and you got too tired, so they let you sit on the wagon.

speaker: interviewee
text: You got into it town. And I says to the man, I'm going to look around and I'll be back to help you. So of course you didn't have the mind to come back and help him, but you had to see what you can do for yourself because you were afraid he will give you away or what happened, because he didn't know you, you don't know him. And he didn't ask much questions because you didn't even-- you didn't offer any voluntary, you know-- you didn't want to tell him who you are or what you come from-- where you come from.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I came to Budapest. And I started looking around and asking people if there is any Jewish synagogue still open or any, any temples or anything where I can go and inquire about something what I can do. So one woman told me, yes, there is somewhere in the synagogue. [NON-ENGLISH], somewhere, it's called [NON-ENGLISH]. There is a small synagogue, and there is some Jews there yet. So I inquired and I started walking in the direction they pointed me. Finally I got there about a couple hours later. And I went into the synagogue, and there was what they call a [NON-ENGLISH] who used to take care of the synagogue, used to keep it clean, sweep it, and keep it in order. So I told him who I am, and I am just escape from a forced labor camp, and my name is so-and-so, and I'm originally from Mukach. Could you give me any idea or help what I can do? So he says to me, I don't know what to tell you, but maybe one night you can stay here in the synagogue. And in the morning, there is a place called [NON-ENGLISH], and there is a Swedish consulate in that street, and they try to give passports and papers to Jewish people under the Swedish protection. And go there and find out what you can do.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure enough, I stayed there overnight and they gave me some tea and a slice of bread. I was in heaven. [SOB] And I came to that place where that Swedish consulate was. And it was kind of dangerous, because was people all around, the watchmen in uniforms, and all that. And I says, I just came to fix some plumbing there. And I had some tools in my hand, in my pocket. And I wasn't cleaned up very well yet. I still was looking like with coal. And he says, I have to-- they summoned me I should come and fix something. So finally they let me in. And when I came in, I told the people, is there anybody I can talk to and tell them the true, the true story. He says, yeah, there is upstairs a man. And you have to go up and you have to wait here.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I waited, and they started looking at me and asking me all kinds of questions. Because in the beginning, they didn't even trust you. They were afraid. So I told them exactly where who I am, my parents who they were, what city I come from.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they have some people from the same town somehow in there, which knew the familiarity of the place. So and I said, Spitz, Spitz, yeah, sounds familiar. I think I know the guy. I think he's OK. You can trust him. He's OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they question me more, and they question me where I was and where my family is. I says, I don't know nothing about my brother or my sister or my parents. I said, I don't know they were taken away to the brick factory in the ghetto. And from there, they put them in these cattle cars, and they took them. I don't know where they took them.

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, I didn't even know. I didn't heard about Auschwitz yet. I didn't know they took them to Auschwitz. Later on I found out that all these cattle cars, all these people were taken to Auschwitz, and they were put in the barracks there. And they separated the men from the women, and the parents from the children. The children were taken in different, right and left, and back and front. They, they tore up the families. It was a terrible situation. The mother didn't know about her kids, and the kids didn't know about their father or about their mother. And it was a terrible situation.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I says, is there anything I can do to help. He says, yeah. If you have guts enough and courage enough, then we'll make you work. We'll put you up to, to help as much as you can. But it's up to you. If they catch you, you're on your own. You're not allowed to tell that we gave you papers and all these things to help. You're not allowed to-- Who said that? Who was that who you spoke to?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was from  the Zionist organization in the, in the Swedish consulate some people. I didn't know who the man was. Later on I found out he was Raoul Wallenberg. He was the one who-- [SOBBING] excuse me. He was the one who saved a lot of people. So he says to me, Harry, you're young enough and you're strong. We'll try to do the best we can. We're going to give you certain papers that your name is Kovac Pista. You're Hungarian, and you're a Nazi. You're a Hungarian Nazi. But you have to dress up in their uniform. And we're going to give you a uniform. And we're going to fix you up with papers, with phony papers. And your name for now will be Kovac Pista. And we're going to give you names and addresses, what to do, and when to go there, and how to go about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I says, I'm going to go by myself? He said, no, no, no. We're going to fix you up, two, three other boys, and you're all going to go like in a group. And you're going to be all dressed like Hungarian Nazis. And you come in, and you, you got to take those people and bring them out and bring them here to the Swedish consul or the Swiss consul and put them in safe-- to safety. So sure, enough we-- Where did you sleep at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: We slept in the, in the, in the, in the basement of the Swedish consulate.
